dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 7 responding for breathing problems at 3009 Riverside Drive.
Hi, I'm Taylor, an EMT with the rescue team. What made you call 911 today?
I can't catch my breath. It started getting really hard to breathe this morning.
Can you tell me your name?
Shelby...
Shelby, can you tell me what year it is?
Shelby, can you tell me what was happening when you started to feel short of breath?
I live with Shelby and we were getting ready for dinner when she said she couldn't breathe.
I've been having trouble breathing all day. I tried my Albuterol from home, but it didn't help.
Have you had any fever or cough?
Yes, I've had a fever all day and coughing a lot too.
Have you been diagnosed with asthma before?
Yes, I've had severe asthma since I was a kid.
Are you allergic to any medications or other substances?
I'm allergic to aspirin.
Do you take any medications regularly?
I use Albuterol when I'm having trouble breathing.
On a scale of 0 to 10, with 10 being the worst difficulty you've ever had, how would you rate your breathing now?
10 - it's the worst it's ever been.
Shelby, we're going to do a few things quickly, okay? First things first, I need you to be alert and aware for us to proceed safely.
Shelby, tell me your name again and the current year to check your responsiveness.
My name is Shelby, and the year is 2020. I'm aware of my surroundings here.
Shelby, your airway is patent and clear. That's a good sign so far.
I just heard wheezing in both lungs. You're breathing fast, overly using your accessory muscles. Let me check your oxygen level now.
Her oxygen saturation is 95% right now on room air. We should consider administering more oxygen for comfort and safety.
Shelby's respiratory rate is 30, and it's a bit shallow. Let me continue the assessment of her breathing pattern while you continue your exam, Taylor.
Her blood pressure is 126 over 100. Her heart rate is 124, fast but strong and regular. Normal now, but let's monitor closely throughout the call.
Shelby, I notice your skin appears pale, warm, and dry. Any shivering or spectral changes lately?
You're alert and oriented to person, place, and time. Your GCS is 15, which indicates you are A&Ox4.
Shelby, your pupils are equal, round, and reactive. We'll continue with the rest of the neurological check once we monitor you through this 12-lead ECG.
Shelby, I'm going to listen to your lungs again. Deep breaths, and let me hear what's going on in there.
Pulse remains at 124 currently, strong and regular. No changes from the initial measurement.
Let me draw blood for a glucose test now. We need to assess for any metabolic stressors or hypoglycemia signs.
Sorry, Shelby, I was not able to obtain a glucose reading per ePCR, but we will proceed with our plan. I'm documenting this in the report as glucose: 0.
Let me check her ETCO2. Measured at 36. Good levels for now with the current level of distress.
Partner, could you perform a 4-lead ECG as per protocol for respiratory distress? Let's ensure her heart status is stable during this.
Implementing the 4-lead ECG as instructed. Letting you visually check the lead positions.
Partner, is the ECG showing any abnormal patterns?
Partner, I think we still need a 12-lead ECG to check for any additional abnormalities or changes.
Performing the 12-lead ECG now. Let me place the leads before moving on to other procedures.
Partner, the ECG is showing sinus tachycardia with possible right atrial enlargement and moderate ST depression. That's notable.
Administering 0.3mg Epinephrine intramuscularly in her right deltoid now. Routine dose for severe wheezing.
Her oxygen saturation is now down to 76%. I'm going to increase the oxygen flow to 12 liters via a non-rebreather mask.
She is engaging her accessory muscles and wheezing is still present. Breathing is still at 30 per minute.
Her pulse increased to 130 right now, which is expected after an epinephrine dose. Must continue monitoring her closely.
Blood pressure is 134 over 100 - still within normal ranges, but on the upper end of normal.
Giving 10mg Dexamethasone intravenously now. This should help with the inflammation and wheezing over the next hour.
Let's wait and recheck her in the next 5 minutes. Breathing is still at 30, and accessory muscle use is ongoing. Pulse oximetry is useful for tracking progress.
Her oxygen improved back up to 95% under the non-rebreather mask-That's a significant improvement, Shelby. Good progress.
Beginning the 2g dose of Magnesium Sulfate IV now. Set up the pump and will monitor the infusion at a steady rate.
Establishing a 20g IV access in Shelby's left antecubital fossa now. This is a secure IV for injection and monitoring.
Let's confirm with the receiving ED that they're aware of the ECG findings and the medications she received. They should be prepared to evaluate the results quickly.
She remains in respiratory distress with ongoing wheezing and work of breathing. Her response to medications is partial but positive.
Shelby, how would you rate your breathing discomfort now on a scale from 0 to 10?
Maybe 8 now. It's better, but not much.
Shel, your vitals now are a pulse of 130, RR 30, BP 134/100, and oxygen back down to 76%. Second 12-lead ECG is in place for tab monitoring during transport.
Shelby, it looks like we need to transport you to St. Mary's for more advanced care and a definitive diagnosis.
I've placed her on the stretcher and secured her harness now. Ready to move. Let's double-check everything.
Yep, everyone's ready. Let me just signal the driver and get ready to start transport in 3... 2... 1.
To the hospital with you, Shel. We'll monitor you the entire trip. Keep those deep breaths going.
Shelby, your airway is still clear and patent, and your work of breathing response has improved slightly with treatment. Just hold on. We've got a few more minutes before arrival.
ED is aware of your vitals and oxygen status. They've confirmed the team is ready upon our arrival.
You're the one doing all the heavy work here, Shel. Just keep it up. Every breath counts. We are almost there.
Eyes on the vitals. Pulse 130, oxygen 76%, and respirations 30. No dramatic changes, but continue to closely monitor.
Shelby, thanks for being so cooperative. Your oxygen improved earlier, and we are hopeful it will hold up for the remainder of the ride.
Driver, recall to slow at the hospital entrance just ahead. Prepare for a smooth unloading at the ED.
All set, Shelby. We've got a great plan in place, and the ED is on alert. Just keep working with us. You're doing a strong job.
Everyone is prepped. Handing off should be smooth. ED has been updated about your level of distress and the treatment we've provided.
Leaving on the stretcher soon. I'll just give a quick visual check on your airway and oxygen mask before offloading.
Just to confirm: she remains in distress, vitals stable but abnormal, and plans are in place for further treatment. All set.
Shel, when we arrive at the ED, they'll take over. Stay strong. You're not alone in this. We're with you until the end.
All systems go. I'm watching the vitals and will inform you of any changes. Relax, we've got this.
Shel, let's keep your positive progress going. Relay this rhythm: deep breath in, then out with control. Hold steady with the support of the team here.
All vitals and equipment checks have occurred. Ready. The final leg of this call is upon us-let's finish this strong.
